era_context: Classical Greece in the early consolidation phase following the Persian Wars. Athens is strengthening its naval dominance and democratic institutions. Sparta remains the leading land power but is increasingly challenged by Athenian ambitions. The Delian League is in its early years of operation. In China, the Spring and Autumn Period is transitioning to the Warring States Period. In India, early Buddhist communities are organizing after the Buddha's death (if dated to this period).

events.title: Cimon's rise to prominence in Athens
events.description: Cimon, son of the famous general Miltiades (victor at Marathon), emerges as the leading political and military figure in Athens during the early 470s BCE. Cimon advocates for continued alliance with Sparta and aggressive action against Persian forces in the eastern Mediterranean. He leads successful naval campaigns against Persian-held territories and becomes the dominant figure in Athenian politics, eclipsing rivals like Themistocles (who is ostracized around this time) and Aristides. Cimon's ascendancy represents a shift toward a more conservative, pro-Spartan faction in Athens.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98–101

events.certainty_note: Cimon's rise is well-attested in later sources, though the exact chronology of his early career is uncertain. He was certainly prominent by the 470s BCE, but the precise year of his emergence as the dominant figure is not specified in surviving sources.

events.title: Naxos revolt and Athenian response
events.description: The island of Naxos, a member of the Delian League, attempts to withdraw from the alliance. Athens, under Cimon's leadership, responds by besieging and subduing Naxos, forcing it to remain in the league. This event marks an early instance of Athens using military force to maintain control over league members, foreshadowing the transformation of the Delian League into an Athenian empire. The date of this event is uncertain; some sources place it around 471 BCE, while others suggest 470 or 469 BCE.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98

events.certainty_note: The Naxos revolt is attested in Thucydides, but the date is uncertain, with sources suggesting 471–469 BCE. Thucydides places it early in the Delian League's history but does not give a precise date.
cross_references: Part of broader pattern of Athenian imperialism

events.title: Continued Persian Wars in the eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Greek forces, primarily Athenian, continue military operations against Persian-held territories in the eastern Mediterranean. Cimon leads campaigns against Persian forces in Thrace, the Hellespont, and Asia Minor. These campaigns are part of the ongoing conflict between Greek city-states and the Persian Empire following the major battles of 480–479 BCE. The exact battles and dates of individual engagements in 471 BCE are not well-documented, but the general pattern of Greek offensive operations is clear from later historical accounts.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book 1.98

events.certainty_note: The general pattern of continued Greek-Persian conflict is well-attested, but specific events in 471 BCE are not clearly documented. The chronology of Cimon's campaigns is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader -470s to -450s period of Greek-Persian conflict

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the Naxos revolt is disputed; it may have occurred in 470, 469, or even 468 BCE rather than 471 BCE. The narrative of Cimon as a consistently pro-Spartan figure is complicated by later evidence showing his policies shifted over time. The extent of Athenian imperialism in the early 470s is debated; some scholars argue it was more gradual than the traditional narrative suggests.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 471 BCE is similar to 470 BCE: moderate for Greece, sparse for other regions. Greek sources are retrospective and often written by authors with political biases (Plutarch favored biographical narrative; Thucydides had his own interpretive framework). The surviving record emphasizes Athenian and Greek perspectives, with minimal information about Persian, Egyptian, or other contemporary civilizations. The year 471 BCE is not particularly well-documented even within Greek history; many events are dated to the 'early 470s' without precision.

graph_edges.note: Cimon's rise is contemporary with Themistocles' ostracism
Naxos revolt and Athenian response foreshadows later Athenian imperialism
Continued Greek-Persian conflict shapes Athenian foreign policy
